title: Consolidate Overlapping Time Blocks
description:
### Objective
Given a collection of closed intervals [start, end], merge all overlapping intervals and return the result sorted by start. If no intervals overlap, return them sorted.

### Function Contract
Implement `merge_intervals(intervals: List[List[int]])` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `intervals`: type `array` (example: `[[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]`).
- Return type: `array`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(n log n)`, space `O(n)`.

### Constraints
- 0 ≤ n ≤ 10^5
- -10^9 ≤ start ≤ end ≤ 10^9
explanation:
Intuition
Merging intervals is fundamentally about ordering and local decisions. Once intervals are sorted by start, any overlap can only occur with the current running interval at the end of our answer. This allows a single linear scan to either extend the last merged interval when an overlap happens or append a new disjoint interval when it does not.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Sort intervals ascending by start
2. Main Algorithm: Maintain a result list; for each interval, if it overlaps with the last merged one, extend the end to max(end); otherwise append a fresh interval
3. Return Result: Return the merged list

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n log n) due to sorting; merge scan is linear
- Space Complexity: O(n) for output

Key Insights
- After sorting, only adjacent comparisons are needed
- Closed intervals overlap when next.start ≤ last.end

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to sort breaks correctness
- Using '<' instead of '≤' misses touching merges

testCases.explanation: Touching intervals merge under closed semantics

hints: Sort by start to make overlaps local
Two intervals overlap if next.start ≤ last.end
Update last.end with max when merging
